tribe_description: A tribe of hands-on gear analysts who evaluate products based on what the label suggests. They generally prioritize hands-on use, equipment and gear.
tribe_definition: A tribe of hands-on gear analysts who evaluate products based on what the label suggests. They generally prioritize hands-on use, equipment and gear.
population_definition: Health-conscious consumers who evaluate supplements, personal care, and wellness products.

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When evaluating a product, they break the listing into testable sub-claims and then try to confirm each with direct, hands-on observations. They treat unverified specs as provisional and elevate any spec-to-experience mismatch because they believe it predicts returns and long-term frustration. In reviews, they cite the specific observation that supports the conclusion (what physically changed, what failed, what could not be done in normal use) rather than relying on overall impressions.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes marketing claims are cheap and only observable evidence predicts ownership outcomes; gap blocks repeatedly showed actual reviews anchored conclusions in concrete physical evidence and called out claim/experience mismatches, while predictions stayed generic and lost the evidentiary move (missing_nuance, multiple gaps).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When trying daily-use gear, they evaluate the ownership loop as the real product: how it docks, charges, stores, cleans, and reassembles under normal conditions. They describe charging and storage as physical rituals with constraints (what must be opened, what position it must sit in, what gets awkward around sinks/counters), because they believe friction here compounds into non-use. In reviews, they will downgrade an item that performs well but forces awkward posture or placement during routine steps.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes small daily rituals decide adoption more than headline performance; gap blocks emphasized device-specific charging geometry/ritual as decisive, and this window adds that they often name the exact access method (including when a tool is needed to pry a cover) rather than vague ‘awkward charging’ language (HIGH, repeated).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When the purchase is a kit, bundle, or multi-function device, they evaluate each component as if bought alone before allowing the bundle to count as a deal. They benchmark each part against a named real-world baseline and state equivalence by dimension (as-good-as vs worse-than) rather than giving a single blended verdict. In reviews, they explicitly identify which component carries the value and which one sets the ceiling on satisfaction.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes bundles hide weak links, so fairness requires component-by-component baselining; gaps showed consistent decomposition and baseline mapping, and this window reinforces that they recombine into a verdict based on each component’s role rather than treating one item as ‘the main product’ (MEDIUM-HIGH, repeated).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When judging long-term value, they foreground replaceability and ecosystem fit as the main cost lever: what consumables wear out first, what standard they match, and how easy it will be to source equivalents later. They treat compatibility with a dominant ecosystem as immediate purchase relief and will say so plainly, because it changes the cost-to-own equation. In reviews, they call out the exact consumable category that is standardized versus locked-in, rather than offering generic ‘replacement parts are available’ reassurance.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes true value is cost-to-own, not price, and that ecosystem-standard consumables are the biggest lever; gaps showed they celebrated non-proprietary replacements when tied to a dominant ecosystem and were precise about which consumable category got that benefit—predictions misassigned the relief (MEDIUM-HIGH).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product uses proprietary charging or uncommon interfaces, they treat it as continuity risk rather than a convenience detail. They model concrete failure modes—lost cable, travel friction, inability to source replacements—and downgrade the product even if core performance is strong. If a kit mixes interface standards across its components, they escalate the critique because the inconsistency itself is taken as evidence of incoherent design and future serviceability trouble.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes interface coherence predicts long-term usability; multiple gaps (including this window) showed they audit standards per component, treat mixed standards as a design-coherence red flag, and tie it to downstream continuity failures—predictions reassigned the issue to the wrong component or flattened it (HIGH, repeated and salient).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When specs or geometry imply different real-world intensity or risk, they convert that inference into a usable protocol rather than leaving it as a vague caution. They explain the mechanism they think is responsible (pressure, seal, opening size, leverage) and give a practical starting procedure that reduces the chance of an unpleasant first session. In reviews, this shows up as step-by-step guidance meant to prevent avoidable discomfort and returns.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes responsible reviews prevent predictable misuse; a gap showed the actual review tied physical design dynamics to increased intensity and provided a specific start-setting protocol, while the prediction offered only generic warnings without mechanism (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When comparing products, they use an equivalence-mapping mindset: what is truly the same, what is a downgrade, and what is an upgrade on a named axis. They expect trade-offs (bulk, complexity, proprietary parts) to be justified by clear functional gains that improve the lived routine, not just feature count. In reviews, they make the comparison structure explicit so readers can translate it to their own baseline.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes shoppers decide relative to a baseline, not in isolation; gaps around baseline brand comparisons and dimension-by-dimension scoring indicate explicit equivalence mapping, while predictions flattened this into standalone evaluation and generic adequacy language (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When a product includes accessories, they judge them by scenario coverage—what specific problems each tip/attachment unlocks—rather than treating them as generic extras. They praise a set when distinct attachments meaningfully expand use for particular needs and call out when the box is padded with redundant pieces. In reviews, they name which attachments matter and why, because they believe accessory utility is part of honest value accounting.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes accessory value is functional coverage, not item count; gap evidence (including this window) showed accessory praise was tied to named attachments that expanded real scenarios, while predictions ignored the specific pieces that served as the reviewer’s positive evidence (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When handling comfort and form factor are central to use, they treat ‘bulkiness’ and ‘unnatural to use’ as integration tests, not subjective nitpicks. They assume an awkward grip, size, or motion will reduce adherence and effectively erase performance advantages, so they foreground handling realism in the verdict. In reviews, they frame ergonomics as a keep-or-abandon predictor: if it doesn’t fit the routine comfortably, it won’t stay in the routine.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes adoption is behavioral, not aspirational; a gap showed the decisive veto was handling form factor as an integration failure, while the prediction substituted performance-consistency framing and omitted the usability veto signal (MEDIUM).

inherent_behavioral_traits.text: When they detect attachment wobble or looseness, they treat it as tolerance/QC evidence and calibrate it against a known comparison standard. Rather than speculating abstractly about durability, they describe how the fit differs from a baseline brand and what that implies for stability during use. In reviews, this becomes a predictive warning: small mechanical play today is read as an early indicator of future annoyance or failure.
inherent_behavioral_traits.rationalization: This tribe believes small mechanical ‘tells’ predict future reliability; gap blocks showed wobble was explicitly anchored to a baseline comparison and used as QC evidence, while predictions turned it into uncalibrated speculation and lost the comparative calibration (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When buying tech or gear they’ll use repeatedly, they’re motivated by confidence that it will keep working with minimal surprise costs or lock-in. They prefer products that remain serviceable through standardized parts, replaceable attachments, and common interfaces, because that reduces future dependency on one brand. Their satisfaction comes from feeling ‘future-proofed’ as much as from day-one performance.
main.rationalization: Gaps indicate a strong underlying need to avoid ecosystem traps (cross-compatible consumables, mixed/proprietary charging treated as long-term risk) rather than merely optimizing upfront value (HIGH).

main.text: They’re driven to catch the hidden ‘daily friction’ costs that don’t show up in spec sheets—how charging, cleaning, docking, and part fit affect sustained use. They experience poor ergonomics and maintenance hassle as a form of product dishonesty because it shifts effort onto the buyer. This is why they spend review attention on mundane routines other shoppers skip.
main.rationalization: Repeated gap emphasis on device-specific charging geometry/ritual and handling integration showed routine friction was decisive, and this window reinforced the preference for concrete ritual details (e.g., when access requires prying) over abstract battery/build notes (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: They’re motivated by protecting other buyers through actionable guidance, not just opinions—turning observations into operating recommendations and warnings. When a design choice changes comfort or safety dynamics, they feel compelled to spell out how to use it correctly and what to avoid. They see this as peer-to-peer accountability that compensates for thin manufacturer guidance.
main.rationalization: Gap evidence showed translation of design physics into an explicit safe-use starting protocol, reflecting a helper/guardian motive beyond generic performance commentary (MEDIUM).

main.text: When choosing between similar options, they’re motivated by products that prove reliability through tight tolerances and coherent design choices rather than feature count. They treat wobble, loose fit, and inconsistent interface decisions as signs the maker cut corners in ways that will surface later as breakage or hassle. This drives them to emphasize mechanical ‘tell’ signs over polished marketing claims.
main.rationalization: Gaps showed wobble/looseness was used as a baseline-calibrated reliability signal and mixed charging standards were treated as a design red flag, while predictions glossed these as minor or missed them (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When shopping for multi-part products, they’re motivated to avoid being misled by the convenience of bundling. They want to know which component meets category expectations and which one will create regret, because they view the weakest-link experience as what owners actually live with. This is why their reviews often read like two (or more) separate evaluations stitched into one value conclusion.
main.rationalization: Gaps repeatedly showed component-by-component benchmarking and explicit weakest-link accounting, and this window highlighted that they resist narratives that crown one component as ‘the main product’ when the other still needs category-equivalence scrutiny (MEDIUM-HIGH).

main.text: When something feels awkward in the hand or bulky in the routine, they’re motivated to prevent ‘optimistic purchases’ that end up unused. They interpret form-factor friction as behavior-shaping: even strong performance won’t matter if the product is unpleasant to handle repeatedly. This makes them unusually sensitive to handling realism compared to spec-driven shoppers.
main.rationalization: A gap highlighted bulkiness/unnatural handling as the core adoption veto that predictions replaced with performance framing, indicating a strong avoidance of non-adoption purchases (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When selecting products for their setup, they aim to minimize long-term ownership friction—replacement parts, charging, and maintenance—so the item stays usable for years. They prefer purchases that won’t strand them when a cable is lost, a tip wears out, or a component breaks. This goal shows up in reviews as forward-looking warnings and ‘future-proofing’ advice tied to concrete failure modes.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gaps highlighted proprietary charging, mixed interface choices, and consumable compatibility as long-term risk levers that predictions ignored or mis-assigned, indicating an outcome focus on sustained usability (MEDIUM-HIGH).

implicit_goals.text: When reviewing bundled or multi-function products, they aim to give readers a component-by-component verdict anchored to real baselines so buyers can judge whether the bundle is truly a deal. They want peers to know which part is as-good-as a known standard, which part is worse-than, and where the real value is coming from. Their reviews are designed to prevent ‘bundle blindness’ where one strong component hides a weak companion device.
implicit_goals.rationalization: The divergence showed a clear goal of decomposing bundles and benchmarking each component against named standards, and this window reinforced that they assign roles to each component rather than letting one dominate the narrative (MEDIUM-HIGH).

implicit_goals.text: When they discover a spec-to-experience mismatch that could affect comfort or safety, they aim to convert it into practical user instructions other shoppers can follow immediately. They explain why the product behaves the way it does and then give a ‘start here’ procedure that reduces the learning curve. They want the next buyer to avoid the unpleasant first-use experiment they had to run.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap showed they tied physical mechanism to intensity and provided a concrete starting protocol, beyond generic ‘be careful’ language (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When choosing long-lasting, high-performance products, they aim to ensure the product integrates smoothly into their existing setup—physically, ergonomically, and infrastructurally. They look for coherent interface choices, stable placement during charging/storage, and handling comfort that survives repetition. In reviews, they frame integration as the difference between a device they keep and one they abandon despite decent core performance.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Window gaps centered on charging workflow realism, mixed-interface incoherence, and bulk/handling fit as decisive integration criteria rather than abstract performance (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When writing reviews, they aim to provide guidance that predicts ownership outcomes rather than just reporting first impressions. They emphasize the failure modes that matter later—compatibility, replaceability, routine friction—and back them with observable evidence and baseline comparisons. Their desired outcome is a reader who can decide with confidence whether the product will stay in a routine or become a drawer item.
implicit_goals.rationalization: The gaps consistently show a predictive, failure-mode orientation (continuity risk, component benchmarking, ritual mechanics) that predictions flattened into generic praise/complaint (MEDIUM).

implicit_goals.text: When shopping, they aim to avoid costly mistakes by uncovering hidden trade-offs a listing won’t highlight, especially those that compound over time. They treat small design inconsistencies (like mixed standards or loose tolerances) as early warning signals and make the downstream consequence explicit. In reviews, they translate those signals into what will likely annoy, break, or cost money later.
implicit_goals.rationalization: Gap evidence showed they escalated mixed interface choices, charging rituals (including prying/tool access), and wobble into long-horizon consequences and calibrated warnings, not generic annoyance notes (MEDIUM).

member_user_characteristics.characteristic_summary: This user is a meticulous, tech-savvy enthusiast who enjoys dissecting products—especially video-game remakes and gadgets—down to their mechanical, visual, and usability components. Their primary motivation is to help fellow consumers make well-informed decisions by providing exhaustive, feature-by-feature comparisons that weigh quality-of-life upgrades, technical performance, and design practicality against shortcomings. They value modern standards, functionality, and user experience, and they structure their reviews with detailed lists and balanced critiques to maximize clarity and utility for others.

category_characteristics.Health & Personal Care: This user is a meticulous, technically minded evaluator who benchmarks products against well-known standards and scrutinizes usability, compatibility, and build quality more than cost. They write methodical, feature-rich reviews to guide others toward reliable, convenient choices, often including practical tips and warnings drawn from hands-on experience. Their primary motivation appears to be helping shoppers avoid functional inconveniences and discover products that integrate smoothly into everyday routines.

user_history_reviews.review_text:
Like Resident Evil 2 and 3 remakes; tons of quality-of-life changes and modern controls. Not just a HD filter added on. Makes the originals had HD remasters obsolete. If you play this and the originals back-to-back it’s a night-and-day difference

--List of Changes:

1. Redone graphics. Everything re-done from the ground up to make it look more modern. No HD filter. Some level redesigned. Character models also changed. I really like the realistic hair animations

2. New dodge Parry system. Able to parry with the knife like RE3 remake. Also able to parry objects enemies throw at you if timed correctly

3. No more tank controls. Able to move and shot at the same time. Combat is much faster/fluid for Leon as well as enemies. Enemies do higher damage and you must be on your toes more by taking advantage of combo attacks and parries. Ammo is more prevalent, more focus on execution of dodge and attacks than stressing out about ammo count

4. Faster gun reload. Makes combat more fluid

5. Weapon Wheel. Able to swap weapons on the fly by selecting from D-pad instead of open-and-close menu

6. Issue Ashley more commands. Instead of telling Ashley to stay like a dog in previous versions, you can now tell her to hide or stay close

7. Auto-save. Game auto saves instead of restarting you at the type writer manual save. Note that this feature is not available in Professional difficulty

8. New enemies types. Some enemies, bosses are re-designed to fit new gameplay

9. Optional stealth combat. Able to crouch and take out enemies from behind. No stealth exclusive levels for Leon, but there is one for Ashley

10. No new item pause menu. Game gives you notification on screen while you continue combat/walking. No more pop up notification that covers the screen, pausing the game; prompting you to press A, X, etc for items you’ve previously collected

11. New collectable. Ramón Salazar figurine. Collect all 16 to unlock a new knife

12. Breakable objects are now highlighted.

13. New puzzles. Puzzles are redesigned. Require more thought, but still easy to figure out

14. More dialogue. Characters have more dialogue. For example The Merchant has more lines. Leon makes more comments/quips to himself. Leon and Ashley talk have more conversations with each other.

15. Able to craft ammo instead of relying on enemy and crate drops

16. Bounty type side mission rewards you gems which you can spend on upgrades

17. Less QTEs. Mainly regulated to escape an enemy holding onto you, and turning cranks. What was once cutscenes are now full on boss battles

18. Side story, ‘Separate Ways’ not included. At least at near launch (no announced future dlc except mercenaries mode)

Game is available on Xbox Gamepass.

Very Bioshock and Wolfenstien inspired. There’s even a underwater elevator segment where the protagonist mentions rapture

Linear first person shooter. You have an inplant in your left hand that does elemental and physical attacks/defense.

There is a slight open world segment like Gears of War 5 or God of War Ragnorok

Plot: Set in alternate post WWII where robot takes over the city in USSR. Most enemies are robot zombie mannequins, or mechanized ball machines with arms sticking out. Main character has frat boy edgy attitude, can be annoying at times. There are 2 endings. Dependent on a single choice you make at the end

--Gameplay:

Movement and gun handling feels very floaty. Not very engaging especially when guns have odd handling and recoil

Very melee focused in the beginning. Swings are way to wide and must focus on timing

Best aspect of combat is the powers plus gun combo when you’re able to chain it together

Powers are on a cooldown system. Able to have 3 on queue, but not able to remove shock for some reason

Enemy placement is bad. They just flood you with random enemies on screen. Not in a good horde mode kinda way, more like a noob player created level in Little Big Planet, Mario maker style game.

Enemies are bullet sponges at times. Can feel tedious at times finishing off enemies

No sprint. Must upgrade walk speed via skill tree

Aside from the sprint upgrade, skill tree gives you good buffs. Can re-spec at anytime . Need to upgrade at upgrade station where this horny robot tries to flirt with you

Loot resource based gameplay. Get resources after defeating enemies to upgrade skill at upgrade station. Need to collect schematics to upgrade weapons

There are environmental puzzle segments that change the pace a bit where you must use your powers to get to next section

Like most first person shooters, the 3d platforming is horrible

Manual save format at save stations. Checkpoints are okay, can be frustrating if you get gameover after taking out dozens of bullet sponge enemies just to be killed by one bringing you back 5-15mins

There are some glitches and sections where you need to soft reset the game.

Up to 15 second load times are present even on PS5

Overall okay game. Very Bioshock and Wolfenstien inspired but the gameplay is not very polished or well thought out. Could have used more development time in my opinion

Remake of Dead Space (2008) game. Not a complete remake like (Resident Evil 2 and 3, or Final Final Fantasy Remake). The ship layout and core gameplay is similar. Mostly some updated visuals and some quality of life additions. There is added story with more dialogue and a new crew member, Kellion who gives commentary on what’s happening through coms.

Wished they updated the core gameplay. Very annoying when you have to change stances to melee, scan area. Don’t you why you can’t just shot then melee right after. The extra step/button press does build tension, but in terms of gameplay fluidity is feels unnecessary.

Game is linear for the most part, but does have a lot of backtracking and side missions. Must find keys/batteries to reach previous inaccessible areas

I had a okay time with it. A lot of great refinements to make it more modern

--List of Changes:

1. Updated visuals

Much higher detail in character models and environments

Way better lighting and particle effects

There’s added color instead of mostly grey and brown color palette

Redone Issac’s model. He’s shorter and put on a little more weight (looks bulkier). Also takes off his helmet form tim-to-time

2. More dialogue

In addition to the new crew member, Issac is no longer a silent protagonist and has some lines

3. No loading screens.

There are some artificial background loading screens when waiting for data to transfer or door to depressurize

4. New objectives / nodes implementation

Some mission objectives have changed (core story remains the same). For example instead of killing mid boss for a key, you have to do some puzzle/re-route power/oxygen from circuit breaker instead.

As you get further on story, you get security clearance to locked areas this time instead of using node resources. Nodes are stickly used for upgrades. The upgrade tree doesn’t have any blank nodes (each upgrade path use ultilized). Nodes are more rare this time around due to these changes

If you get lost and can’t figure out what to do, you must use guides for remake and not the original

5. Guns tied to story

Guns are unlocked while you progress through the story instead of searching for blueprints or schematics. Still need schematics to upgrade weapons

6. Free flow gravity segments

Now able to move wherever you want in gravity segments instead of pointing at area and launch off (superman swimming pool style). Gives more exploration to the levels, but also higher chance of getting lost ??

7. Side missions

New added side missions. More for getting new items, collectables than story. Side mission tracking has similar UI to Dead Space 3

8. Backtrack anytime*

Except for ending portion of the game, you can backtrack anytime for collectables and side missions. In original game once you finish a chapter you’re are locked out of content when moving onto the next chapter. This is no longer the case

9. Haptic Feedback on PS5 Controller

Mostly done in beginning (for first impressions) then it drops off more as you progress. Similar to GOW Ragnarök

10. Accessibility options

Auto-aim, stick sensitivity/dead zones, auto qte, show warning when pop up scare is about to arrive, toggle to sprint instead of hold, subtitle text customization, color blind mode, etc

11. New secret ending

Done by clearing New game +

Port of a Wii game with some added extras. The core experience/main campaign is the same with main differences being the epilogue chapter post game, added mini games, and some quality of life changes.

No online multiplayer, local only

Still a fun game and one of the best side scrolling Kirby game in the franchise. If you haven’t played Kirby Forgotten Land, I’d consider getting that instead.

--List of changes:

1. Graphics. Updated visuals. Less pixelated. Runs at 60fps

2. Magolar Epilogue.

Additional chapter after you beat the game with new levels. You play as Magolar who is stripped of all his powers and on a journey to get them back. You gain them back through points you spend on a skill tree.

This chapter can be completed in 2 hours or so. Levels are more creative than original campaign especially the boss battles

3. Merry Magoland.

Can play compilation of mini-games across Kirby franchise.

Playing mini-games earn you stamps. Able to cash in stamps for masks which you can use in main campaign

4. Helper Magolar.

Assist mode (can turn on or off) during main campaign, picks you up when falling like Lakitu's in Mario Kart.

Also gives you free potions when notice you struggling.

Note: not available in bonus epilogue chapter

5. New copy abilities: Mech Kirby and Sand Kirby

6. New move sets for returning copy abilities

7. Dodge move added. Initiated when blocking like Smash Bros game

8. Can use items or backup ability

9. Able to use thumbstick instead of just D-pad (like wii version). Only able to move in 8 directions

10. There’s an emote or taunt button

AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.product_description: Water Dental Flosser Cordless for Teeth - Nicwell 4 Modes Dental Oral Irrigator, Portable and Rechargeable IPX7 Waterproof Powerful Battery Life Water Teeth Cleaner Picks for Home Travel
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.review_text:
--Toothbrush

Toothbrush is as good as Sonic Care. Way better than manual toothbrush, get that dentist feeling clean if you’ve never used it before.

May have less options on strength and pulse variety depending on what version of Sonic Care you have.

Uses same head attachment as Sonic Care. Glad there’s no propriety brush head. Very easy to purchase more when you run out.

Charges on bottom via USB Type C. Cannot stand straight when charging. Must pull out rubber water resistant cover with pen or coin, and lay it on it’s side to charge.

--Water Flosser

Water flosser is like a portable WaterPik, but is bulky and not as natural to use. The orthodontic and tongue cleaner tips are a very nice addition.

Water pressure is a bit stronger than standard WaterPik head due to the small opening. Recommend you put it on Soft setting first

Head attachment is a bit loose compared to WaterPik. Wobbles when you touch it slightly.

Propriety head and charger. Have no idea why they didn’t go for USB Type C like for the toothbrush

Overall, very decent bundle. Toothbrush is very similar to Sonic Care brand but has odd charging method. Water flosser a bit strong, and must use propriety changing cable (careful not to lose it)
AGMWACNMAG74AXBF7IJ22IOZSZPA.user_history_review:
This kit ended up being a “two products in one box” situation for me. The water flosser itself does the job: on the first few uses it was obvious it’s getting along the gumline and between tight contacts better than a rinse, and it’s consistent night to night. It’s also stronger than I expected, and the smaller opening on the standard tip seems to concentrate the stream—start on Soft unless you want a rude first session, then step up once you know how it behaves.

Where I’m less impressed is the day-to-day routine stuff. The head on mine has a bit of wobble when seated; it still works, but that kind of loose fit reads like “this will get worse over time.” Tip compatibility is a big deal for me, and I was relieved it takes the common-style replacement tips (not some weird one-off you can’t source later).

Charging is the other mixed bag: the flosser is USB‑C, but the toothbrush in the bundle uses a proprietary cable. That inconsistency is exactly how you end up with one device dead because you lost the wrong cord. On top of that, the rubber charge cover is tight and the unit can’t really stand nicely while charging, so it tends to lay on its side on the counter.

Net: strong cleaning performance and good power, but the charging/fit details keep it from feeling “set and forget.”
